cards.grammarNote: Present tense prefix for 'I' (أنا) = أـ (a-). So كتب (the root k-t-b) → أكتب (aktub = I write). This prefix applies to all verbs: أذهب (I go), أعمل (I work), أدرس (I study).
Present tense prefix for 'he' (هو) = يـ (y-). يكتب (yaktub = he writes). This same prefix is used for 'they (m.)': يكتبون (yaktubun = they write).
Present tense prefix for 'she' (هي) = تـ (t-). Also used for 'you (m.)': تكتب (taktub). Context and the stated pronoun distinguish between 'she writes' and 'you (m.) write.'
Present tense prefix for 'we' (نحن) = نـ (n-). نكتب (naktub = we write). Easy to remember: نحن starts with ن, and so does the verb prefix.
Note تتكلم for 'she speaks' — both the prefix تـ AND the verb starts with تـ, so you get two تـ: ت (prefix) + تكلم (verb) = تتكلم.

cards.front: We go to the office every day.

cards.grammarNote: Arabic present tense covers both 'I learn' and 'I am learning.' There is no separate progressive form. أتعلّم means both.

cards.front: From root ك-ت-ب: book, office, writer, library
cards.grammarNote: Arabic verbs share roots (جذور, judur) with related nouns. The root ك-ت-ب (k-t-b) relates to writing: كتب (he wrote), يكتب (he writes), كتاب (book), مكتب (office/desk), كاتب (writer), مكتبة (library). Learning roots multiplies vocabulary.
